informal_statement.text: Given a binary search tree whose in-order traversal is strictly ascending and two keys both present in the tree, return the key of the lowest node that has both keys in its subtree.
preconditions: the input tree is a valid BST
both query keys are present in the tree
required_properties: the returned key appears in the tree
both query keys are in the subtree rooted at the returned key
no proper descendant of the returned key has both query keys in its subtree
edge_cases: both keys equal (returned key equals them)
one key is an ancestor of the other
keys on opposite sides of the root
tree with only a single path

semantic_units.description: BST precondition: the input tree is a valid BST.
common_failure_modes: omitting the BST precondition and solving general-tree LCA
assuming strict ordering within equal keys

semantic_units.description: Presence precondition: both query keys are present in the tree.
common_failure_modes: returning None silently when a key is missing

semantic_units.description: Ancestor property: both query keys lie in the subtree rooted at the returned key.
common_failure_modes: returning any common ancestor instead of the lowest one

semantic_units.description: Lowest: no proper descendant of the returned node contains both keys.
common_failure_modes: using LCA of general trees (parent-pointer style) semantics

semantic_units.description: Key equality case: when p == q, the LCA is that same key.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (t : Tree) (p q a : Int), IsBst t → HasKey t p → HasKey t q → lcaBst t p q = some a → ∃ sub : Tree, IsSubtree sub t ∧ (match sub with | Tree.nil => False | Tree.node _ k _ => k = a) ∧ HasKey sub p ∧ HasKey sub q
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When the input is a BST containing both query keys, the returned key is the root of a subtree containing both queries.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (t : Tree) (p q a : Int), IsBst t → HasKey t p → HasKey t q → lcaBst t p q = some a → ∀ (sub : Tree), IsProperSubtree sub t → (match sub with | Tree.nil => True | Tree.node _ k _ => k = a → False) → HasKey sub p → HasKey sub q → False
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When the input is a BST containing both query keys, no proper subtree that is not rooted at the returned key contains both queries and sits strictly below the LCA.

reference_obligations.lean_statement: ∀ (t : Tree) (p : Int), IsBst t → HasKey t p → lcaBst t p p = some p
reference_obligations.nl_gloss: When the input is a BST containing the key, the LCA of a key with itself is that key.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
structure BST (α : Type) (lt : α → α → Prop) (key : α → α) : Prop :=
  (inorder_strict_asc : True) -- placeholder for the BST invariant; to be refined by the surrounding development
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Assume a BST structure with an invariant capturing that its in-order traversal is strictly ascending.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
inductive Tree (α : Type) : Type
| empty : Tree α
| node : Tree α → α → Tree α → Tree α

def inorder : Tree α → List α
| Tree.empty => []
| Tree.node l x r => inorder l ++ [x] ++ inorder r
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Define the binary tree and its in-order traversal.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: def present (t : Tree α) (k : α) : Prop := k ∈ inorder t
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Define when a key is present in the tree via membership in the in-order traversal.

generated_obligations.lean_statement: def subtree (t : Tree α) (u : Tree α) : Prop := ∃ l r x, t = Tree.node l x r ∧ (u = l ∨ u = r ∨ u = t) -- placeholder; refine to the intended 'u is a subtree of t' relation
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Define the subtree relation used to talk about nodes whose subtrees contain both keys.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
def hasBothInSubtree (t : Tree α) (k1 k2 : α) : Prop :=
  ∃ u : Tree α, subtree t u ∧ present u k1 ∧ present u k2
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Define the property that there exists some subtree of t containing both keys.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
def lowestNodeWithBoth (t : Tree α) (k1 k2 : α) : Option α :=
  -- intended: choose the node whose subtree contains both keys and is lowest (closest to leaves)
  -- placeholder definition; to be implemented/connected to the algorithm in the surrounding development
  none
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: Define the intended output: the key of the lowest node whose subtree contains both keys.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem lowestNodeWithBoth_some_iff
  (t : Tree α) (k1 k2 : α) :
  lowestNodeWithBoth t k1 k2 ≠ none ↔ hasBothInSubtree t k1 k2 := by
  -- proof obligation: connect the option result to existence of such a node
  admit
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The algorithm returns some key exactly when there exists a node whose subtree contains both keys.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem lowestNodeWithBoth_correct_subtree
  (t : Tree α) (k1 k2 : α) (res : α)
  (h : lowestNodeWithBoth t k1 k2 = some res) :
  ∃ u : Tree α, subtree t u ∧ present u k1 ∧ present u k2 ∧ res ∈ inorder u := by
  admit
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: If the algorithm returns res, then res is the key of a node whose subtree contains both keys.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem lowestNodeWithBoth_lowest_property
  (t : Tree α) (k1 k2 : α) (res : α)
  (h : lowestNodeWithBoth t k1 k2 = some res) :
  ∀ u : Tree α,
    subtree t u →
    present u k1 →
    present u k2 →
    (∃ v : Tree α, subtree u v ∧ res ∈ inorder v) := by
  admit
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: For any node whose subtree contains both keys, the returned node is at least as low (i.e., is contained in the subtree chain of any other such node).

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem lowestNodeWithBoth_unique
  (t : Tree α) (k1 k2 : α) (res1 res2 : α)
  (h1 : lowestNodeWithBoth t k1 k2 = some res1)
  (h2 : lowestNodeWithBoth t k1 k2 = some res2) :
  res1 = res2 := by
  admit
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: The lowest node with both keys is unique in terms of its key, so the algorithm’s result is well-defined.

generated_obligations.lean_statement:
theorem lowestNodeWithBoth_exists_from_present
  (t : Tree α) (k1 k2 : α)
  (h1 : present t k1) (h2 : present t k2) :
  hasBothInSubtree t k1 k2 := by
  -- witness the whole tree as a subtree
  admit
generated_obligations.nl_gloss: If both keys are present in the tree, then there exists at least one node (the root) whose subtree contains both keys.
